skill.name: writing-page-layout
skill.description: Guides AI assistants to create modular Next.js page layouts with short page files and separate type definitions.
skill.summary: Create concise, modular Next.js page layout files.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks and a weak-crypto pattern, but review found no executable code or cryptographic implementation in SKILL.md. Lines 8 and 14 use inline Markdown code spans, and line 3 is plain frontmatter text. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, network access, or command execution intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown inline code spans around page.tsx and srs/app/types. They do not execute commands and contain no user-input injection path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains only Markdown instructions. The backticks are formatting markers, not Ruby or shell execution syntax.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Crypto Pattern
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm location is the frontmatter description text. No cryptographic code, algorithm selection, hashing, or encryption behavior appears at this location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a natural-language description. The weak-crypto signal is a substring match, with no supporting semantic evidence.

content.value_statement: Next.js page files can become hard to maintain when layout logic, types, and components are mixed together. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to produce short, modular page layouts with separated type definitions.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of Next.js app router page layout files.
Promotes modular page composition instead of large page files.
Sets a maximum target of 40 lines per page layout.
Directs type and interface definitions into a separate types file.
Supports TSX page layout work for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
limitations: Does not include reusable component templates or implementation examples.
Does not validate that generated code compiles in a specific project.
Mentions the path srs/app/types, which may need project-specific correction.
Does not define styling, routing, data loading, or accessibility rules.

use_cases.title: Create a clean app router page
use_cases.description: Generate a concise page.tsx file that composes imported sections and keeps type definitions separate.

use_cases.title: Refactor an oversized page file
use_cases.description: Split layout concerns into smaller modules while keeping the page entry point under the stated line target.

use_cases.title: Standardize project page structure
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent page layout pattern across multiple Next.js routes in a shared codebase.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Page Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new route page.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Next.js page.tsx layout for a dashboard route. Keep the page file modular and under 40 lines. Put types and interfaces in a separate types file.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor an Existing Page
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a page file that has grown too large.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Next.js page.tsx into a modular layout. Keep the page entry file under 40 lines and move type definitions out of the page file.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Route Family Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a consistent pattern for related pages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Define a modular page layout pattern for three related Next.js app routes. Use concise page.tsx files and place shared interfaces in a separate types module.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Project Constraints
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with existing imports and conventions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this page layout to match our existing Next.js app structure. Preserve current imports where possible, keep page.tsx under 40 lines, and separate all interfaces into the project types folder.

output_examples.input: Request for a product overview page in a Next.js app route.
output_examples.output: A short page layout plan that composes header, summary, metrics, and activity sections, with page-specific interfaces moved into a types module.

output_examples.input: Existing page.tsx file contains layout, type definitions, and repeated section markup.
output_examples.output: A refactoring outline that keeps the route entry point concise, extracts reusable sections, and separates interface definitions from layout code.

output_examples.input: Team wants a repeatable pattern for multiple marketing pages.
output_examples.output: A consistent layout structure using modular section components, concise page files, and a shared location for page data types.

best_practices: Provide the current page structure and the expected route purpose before asking for changes.
Confirm the correct project path for shared types before applying the generated layout.
Run the project formatter and type checker after generating or refactoring files.
anti_patterns: Do not place complex business logic directly inside page.tsx.
Do not keep large interface blocks in the page layout file.
Do not force the 40-line target when clarity requires a small exception.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create concise, modular Next.js page layout files for app router projects.

faq.question: Does it generate complete applications?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on page layout structure, not full application architecture or deployment.

faq.question: Can it work with existing code?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the existing page file and ask the assistant to refactor it using the skill constraints.

faq.question: Does it require a specific styling framework?
faq.answer: No. The skill does not specify Tailwind, CSS modules, or any other styling system.

faq.question: Why separate types from page.tsx?
faq.answer: Separating types keeps the route entry file easier to scan and maintain.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file contains only Markdown guidance and no executable code or prompt injection attempt.
